---
title: "Data Simulations in R<br> `r emo::ji('game_die')`"
author: "S. Mason Garrison"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: "../slides.css"
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightLines: true
      highlightStyle: solarized-light
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      slideNumberFormat: ""
---

```{r child = "../setup.Rmd"}
```

```{r packages, echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(MASS)
library(openintro)
library(tidymodels)
library(DT)
library(emo)
library(viridis)
set.seed(1234)
```

class: middle

# Learning Goals

---

## Learning Goals

By the end of this session, you will be able to...

- Generate random numbers from various distributions (normal, uniform, Poisson)
- Use `set.seed()` for reproducible simulations
- Create simulated datasets with quantitative and categorical variables
- Use simulations to develop intuition about statistical distributions
- Apply `replicate()` for repeated simulation experiments

---

class: middle

# Why Simulate Data?

---

## Why Simulate Data?

.alert[Imagine you're designing a psychology experiment, but you haven't collected any data yet. How do you know if your study will actually work?]

--

- Maybe you want to know: "If the true effect size is *d* = 0.5, will my sample of 30 participants be enough to detect it?"

--

- Or maybe you want to practice your analysis pipeline *before* you spend months in the lab.

--

- Simulation lets you create artificial data that behaves like real data — so you can plan, test, and build intuition.

--

.pull-left[
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/data scientist at work.png")
```
]

.pull-right[
.tip[
Simulation is one of the most practical skills in a data scientist's toolkit. You'll use it for power analysis, testing code, and understanding statistical concepts.
]
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# Generating Random Numbers

---

## The Normal Distribution: `rnorm()`

.question[
If you measured the exam scores of every introductory psychology student at this university, what would the distribution look like?
]

--

- Probably something close to a **normal distribution** — a bell curve centered around some average.

--

- R can generate data that looks just like that, using `rnorm()`.

---

## `rnorm()`: Start Simple

Let's generate 5 random numbers from a standard normal distribution:

```{r echo=FALSE}
set.seed(5)
```

```{r}
rnorm(5)
```

--

.midi[
Notice how the values cluster around 0? That's because the default mean is 0, and the default standard deviation is 1.
]

---

## `rnorm()`: Name Your Arguments

It's clearer to specify the arguments explicitly:

```{r echo=FALSE}
set.seed(515)
```

```{r}
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
```

--

- `n` = how many values to generate
- `mean` = the center of the distribution
- `sd` = how spread out the values are

---

## `rnorm()`: Make It Meaningful

.question[
Suppose exam scores in your class have a mean of 75 and a standard deviation of 10. How would you simulate 5 students' scores?
]

--

```{r}
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 75, sd = 10)
```

--

.midi[
These look like plausible exam scores! They're scattered around 75, mostly within about 10 points of the mean — exactly what we'd expect.
]

---

## Visualizing `rnorm()` Output

.pull-left[
```{r norm-plot, echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "100%"}
set.seed(1234)
observations <- rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
observations_df <- data.frame(
  y = dnorm(observations),
  x = observations
)

ggplot(data.frame(x = c(-4, 4)), aes(x)) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, n = 101) +
  geom_point(
    data = observations_df,
    aes(y = y, x = x),
    color = viridis(7, option = "D")[3],
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "5 Draws from a Standard Normal",
    x = "Value",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
]

.pull-right[
- The curve shows the theoretical normal distribution.
- The .hand[.light-blue[colored dots]] are our 5 simulated values.
- Each time we run `rnorm()`, we get *different* dots — that's the randomness!
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

## But Wait — Reproducibility!

.question[
If random numbers are different every time, how can we share our results with others and get the same answer?
]

---

## `set.seed()`: Same Randomness Every Time

```{r}
set.seed(16)
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
```

--

Run it again with the **same seed**:

```{r}
set.seed(16)
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
```

--

.hand[.light-blue[Identical!]]

---

## Without `set.seed()`, Results Change

```{r}
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
```

--

```{r}
rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
```

--

.tip[
Always use `set.seed()` at the top of your script when doing simulations. Pick any number you like — it's just a starting point for the random number generator.
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# Beyond the Normal: Other Distributions

---

## Uniform Distribution: `runif()`

.question[
What if every value in a range is equally likely — like reaction times that are randomly jittered between 0 and 500 ms?
]

--

.pull-left[
```{r}
set.seed(1235)
runif(n = 5, min = 0, max = 500)
```

- `min` and `max` define the range
- Every value in that range is equally probable
]

.pull-right[
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "100%"}
set.seed(1235)
observations <- runif(n = 5, min = 0, max = 500)
observations_df <- data.frame(
  y = rep(1/500, length(observations)),
  x = observations
)

ggplot(data.frame(x = c(-50, 550)), aes(x)) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dunif, args = list(min = 0, max = 500), n = 101
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = observations_df,
    aes(y = y, x = x),
    color = viridis(7, option = "D")[5],
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Uniform Distribution (0 to 500)",
    x = "Value",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
]

---

## Poisson Distribution: `rpois()`

.question[
How many times does a participant press a button in a 30-second window? How many errors does a student make on a task? These are **count data** — and the Poisson distribution models them.
]

--

.pull-left[
```{r}
set.seed(123)
rpois(n = 5, lambda = 2)
```

- `lambda` = the average count (mean)
- Values are always whole numbers (0, 1, 2, ...)
]

.pull-right[
```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "100%"}
set.seed(123)
observations <- rpois(n = 5, lambda = 2)
observations_df <- data.frame(
  x = observations,
  y = dpois(observations, lambda = 2)
)

x_values <- seq(0, 10, by = 1)
poisson_df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  y = dpois(x_values, lambda = 2)
)

ggplot(poisson_df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_bar(
    stat = "identity",
    fill = viridis(7, option = "D")[5]
  ) +
  geom_bar(
    data = observations_df,
    aes(x = x, y = after_stat(count) / sum(after_stat(count))),
    stat = "count",
    fill = viridis(7, option = "D")[1],
    alpha = 1,
    position = "identity",
    width = 0.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Poisson Distribution (Lambda = 2)",
    x = "Count",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```
]

---

## How Lambda Shapes the Poisson

.midi[
As lambda increases, the distribution shifts right and becomes more symmetric — it starts to look normal!
]

```{r echo=FALSE, fig.align = "center", out.width = "75%"}
set.seed(123)
lambdas <- c(1, 3, 5, 10)
data_list <- lapply(lambdas, function(l) {
  data.frame(x = rpois(1000, l), lambda = paste("Lambda =", l))
})
poisson_df <- do.call(rbind, data_list)
poisson_df$lambda <- factor(poisson_df$lambda,
                             levels = paste("Lambda =", lambdas))

ggplot(poisson_df, aes(x = x, fill = lambda)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "count", show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~lambda, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    title = "Poisson Distributions at Different Lambda Values",
    x = "Count",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

.footnote[Notice how the distribution becomes more bell-shaped as lambda grows — a useful property to remember!]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# Simulating Categorical Data with `rep()`

---

## Why Do We Need `rep()`?

.alert[You're setting up a between-subjects experiment with two conditions: **control** and **treatment**. You need to create group labels for your participants.]

--

- `rep()` repeats values in patterns — perfect for creating experimental conditions!

---

## `rep()`: The Basics

.question[
What's the difference between `each` and `times`?
]

--

```{r}
rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = 3)
```

.midi[`each = 3`: repeat each element 3 times before moving to the next]

--

```{r}
rep(c("control", "treatment"), times = 3)
```

.midi[`times = 3`: repeat the whole sequence 3 times]

---

## `rep()`: More Options

Unequal group sizes:

```{r}
rep(c("control", "treatment"), times = c(2, 4))
```

--

Fixed total length:

```{r}
rep(c("control", "treatment"), length.out = 5)
```

--

.tip[
`each` and `times` can be combined! `rep(c("A","B"), each = 2, times = 3)` gives 12 elements — useful for factorial designs.
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# Building Simulated Datasets

---

## Putting It Together: A Simple Experiment

.question[
You're simulating an experiment where two groups (control vs. treatment) complete a reaction time task. Under the null hypothesis, there's no group difference. What would that data look like?
]

--

```{r}
set.seed(42)
data.frame(
  group = rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = 3),
  reaction_time = rnorm(n = 6, mean = 250, sd = 30)
)
```

--

.midi[
Both groups draw from the *same* distribution (mean = 250 ms, sd = 30 ms). Any differences are just random noise.
]

---

## Now Add a Real Effect

What if the treatment group is genuinely faster?

```{r}
set.seed(42)
data.frame(
  group = rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = 3),
  reaction_time = rnorm(n = 6, mean = c(250, 220), sd = 30)
)
```

--

.midi[
Now the control group draws from a mean of 250, and the treatment group from 220. The `mean` argument recycles: it alternates between the two values, matching our `each = 3` pattern.
]

---

## A More Complex Design

.midi[
A 2 (group) x 3 (task difficulty) factorial design:
]

```{r}
set.seed(42)
data.frame(
  group = rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = 3),
  difficulty = rep(c("easy", "medium", "hard"), times = 2),
  reaction_time = rnorm(n = 6, mean = c(200, 250, 300), sd = 25)
)
```

--

.midi[
The `mean` vector recycles across all 6 rows, creating different expected reaction times for each difficulty level.
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# Repeated Simulations with `replicate()`

---

## Why Repeat a Simulation?

.alert[Running a simulation once tells you what *could* happen. Running it 1,000 times tells you what *tends* to happen.]

--

.question[
If you flip a coin 10 times, you might get 7 heads. But if you repeat that experiment 1,000 times, you'll see that the average is close to 5. That's the power of repetition!
]

---

## `replicate()`: The Basics

`replicate()` runs the same expression multiple times and collects the results.

```{r}
set.seed(16)
replicate(
  n = 3,
  expr = rnorm(n = 5, mean = 0, sd = 1),
  simplify = FALSE
)
```

---

## `replicate()`: Simulating Datasets

.hand[.light-blue[This is where it gets powerful.]]

We can generate entire datasets over and over:

.small[
```{r}
set.seed(16)
sim_data <- replicate(
  n = 3,
  expr = data.frame(
    group = rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = 3),
    score = rnorm(n = 6, mean = c(75, 80), sd = 10)
  ),
  simplify = FALSE
)
```
]

--

.question[
Why would you want 3 (or 3,000!) copies of the same experiment? Think about it before the next slide...
]

---

## Peeking at Our Simulated Datasets

```{r}
sim_data[[1]]
```

--

```{r}
sim_data[[2]]
```

--

.midi[
Same structure, different random values. Each one is like running the experiment again in a parallel universe.
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

class: middle

# A Preview of What's Next

---

## Simulating Correlated Variables

So far, every variable we've simulated has been **independent**. But real psychological data has *structure*.

--

.question[
If a student scores high on a midterm, what do you expect on the final? Probably also high — the variables are **correlated**.
]

--

```{r}
set.seed(123)
sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1), nrow = 2)
correlated_scores <- mvrnorm(n = 5, mu = c(0, 0), Sigma = sigma)
colnames(correlated_scores) <- c("midterm", "final")
correlated_scores
```

---

## What Does `mvrnorm()` Do?

- `mu` = the means of each variable
- `Sigma` = a **covariance matrix** that encodes how the variables relate

--

```{r}
sigma
```

.midi[
The 0.5 off-diagonal values mean our two variables have a positive correlation of 0.5 — knowing one gives you some information about the other.
]

--

.tip[
Next time (in d31), we'll go deeper: building full correlation structures, using simulation for power analysis, and understanding the Central Limit Theorem — all through simulation.
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---

# Sources

- Ariel Muldoon's [tutorial](https://github.com/aosmith16/simulation-helper-functions)
- Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel's Data Science in a Box ([link](https://datasciencebox.org/))

---

class: middle

# Summary: Learning Goals Achieved

---

## What We've Learned

Today, you should now be able to...

.pull-left[
### Concepts
- ✅ Why simulation matters for experiment planning
- ✅ How distributions (normal, uniform, Poisson) shape data
- ✅ Why reproducibility requires `set.seed()`
]

.pull-right[
### Skills
- ✅ Generate random data with `rnorm()`, `runif()`, `rpois()`
- ✅ Create group labels with `rep()`
- ✅ Build simulated datasets with multiple variables
- ✅ Repeat simulations with `replicate()`
]

---

class: middle

# Wrapping Up...

---
